text: I have completed the mastectomy and now entering into the axilla.
First step would be to separate the whole of the breast tissue from the pectoralis major muscle.
As we can see counter traction by an assistant helps in separating the breast tissue from the pectoralis major muscle.
As I keep on separating the tissue, the pectoralis minor
comes into picture.
Here I am separating the tissue from the pectus minor.
Once the tissue gets separated then I am going to separate it from the serratus anterior.
Now moving superiorly I am going to complete the separation of the tissue from the pectoralis major muscle.
As we can see the intercostal brachial nerve is being cut now.
When you are dividing the fat, you will have a giveaway sensation.
That means you have entered into the clavipectoral fascia.
Now we can see the axillary vein is coming into the picture.
The position of the three retractors here is very important in order to give a proper counter traction and help dissection with the coterie.
First I would like to go near the chest wall.
As we can see Bell's nerve is coming into the picture.

Gentle dissection close to the nerve ensures that I have separated it from the rest of the axillary pack and the nodes and then I gradually push the nerve onto the chest wall so that it doesn't come near my dissection.

now i am going to separate the tissue from the inferior border of the axillary vein next important structure in this part of the dissection is the thoracodorsal pedicle which contains the arterial supply venous drainage and the nerve supply to the latissimus dorsi muscle
As we can see the thoracodorsal pedicle is coming into the picture.
I am going to separate all the tissues from the medial and then anterior surface of the pedicle.

now as we can see the thoracodorsal pedicle gives a number of branches on the lateral aspect it's important to coagulate these properly for two reasons one they can lead on to bleeding which can make the operative field very messy second if you are not careful in coagulation then the vessel itself might get thrombosed
and the whole purpose of saving the pedicle will not get served.

Now, once I have preserved the two most important structures in the dissection, that is the Bell's nerve and the thoracodorsal pedicle, I am going to separate the tissue lateral to the thoracodorsal pedicle from the axillary vein superiorly and the latissimus dorsi muscle
posteriorly.

lateral edge of the latissimus dorsi muscle is the lateral limit of the dissection so i'm going to take all the tissue and separate it from the edge of the latissimus dorsi muscle as seen here this completes the level one and two lymph node clearance
Now we are going into the inter pectoral space to clear these lymph nodes.
I generally hold the pectoralis major muscle with the two babcocks and lift it up.
This creates the space between the major and the minor muscle.
Important is to stick to the undersurface of the pectoralis major muscle and go on dissecting till you find the
medial edge of the muscle.
Once I find the medial edge of the muscle I place a right angled retractor in order to provide a better counter traction.
Now I complete the dissection on the under surface of the pectoralis major.
sweep all the tissue down onto the pectrolase minor as we can see the pectrolase minor is being separated from the tissue
now i am going to separate already separated tissue from the anterior surface of the pectoralis minor continuation of the same above will complete the inter pectoral nodal dissection
Moving on to the level 3 axillary clearance.
I retract the pectoralis major with the right angle and define the medial boundary of the pectoralis minor muscle.
Place a retractor on the pectoralis minor medial border and then dissect the tissue from the chest wall as well as from the axillary vein.

This part of the dissection is little difficult to demonstrate because the space is limited and majority of the times we are not able to focus on the tissue either the hands or the coterie is being seen.
Once the tissue is separated from the
axillary vein and from the chest wall.
I generally sweep all the tissue beneath the pectoralis minor muscle and then from the lateral side I dissect it out.

this we can see is the axillary vein this is our thoracodorsal pedicle we can see the pulsation artery vein and the nerve this is our bell's nerve so this thoracodorsal pedicle has divided into two branches this is the branch to latissimus dorsi and this is the branch to chest wall this is our latissimus dorsi muscle
and i have cleared the inter-pectoral group that is level two and the apex also here i have reached up to the origin of the sub-cleared wing i go down here this is level three accidental clearance
